title: AI capex by company
question: Who is actually paying for the AI buildout, company by company - how big is each one's capex, how fast is it growing, and is each company funding the build out of its own cash or out of someone else's balance sheet?
finding: It is not a broad boom - it is five companies. Across 45 US-listed filers spanning the whole AI value chain, latest-fiscal-year capex totals about $520bn, but the top five (the hyperscalers - Amazon $132bn, Alphabet $91bn, Meta $70bn, Microsoft $65bn, Oracle $56bn) are 79% of it, Amazon alone a quarter, and their combined capex grew +73% in one year ($238bn -> $413bn). The rest of the chain barely spends: fabless silicon (Nvidia 2.8% capex/revenue, +$97bn FCF) and software (Palantir, Salesforce <2%) monetize the build rather than fund it. Following the cash settles the bigger question: in AGGREGATE the build pays for itself - the 45 names generated $910bn of operating cash against $520bn capex, a +$390bn surplus, and still returned $182bn to shareholders (against $176bn net new debt); even the five biggest spenders self-fund ($588bn hyperscaler OCF vs $413bn capex). So 'AI is a credit bubble' fails at the aggregate. The fragility is the funding, not the magnitude, and it is narrow: the debt is a CHOICE for the core, a NECESSITY for the edge. Alphabet added $37bn of net new debt - the same as Oracle - but Alphabet returned $46bn to holders and earned +$73bn FCF (cheap-debt-plus-buyback optimization), while Oracle returned nothing and burned -$24bn FCF (funding the gap). The tell that separates them is returning cash WHILE building: 32 of 45 self-fund (21 also return cash), only 4 are genuinely debt-funded (Oracle + neoclouds + utilities). The war chest agrees - Nvidia holds 7.5 years of capex in cash, the core ~1.3, Oracle 0.6, CoreWeave 0.3. Verdict conditional: the systemic risk is not the aggregate dollar figure; it is a small, nameable set (Oracle and the neoclouds) funding the build with outside money (debt for Oracle/CoreWeave, equity raises for the smaller neoclouds) while burning cash on the thinnest cushions - exactly the financing-refusal trigger of studies 27 and 30, now with names and numbers. Source: SEC EDGAR XBRL, gross PP&E capex + balance-sheet cash/debt + buybacks, latest full fiscal year; net new borrowing measured as the YoY change in the debt stock (not the CP-inflated issuance line). Caveat: US filers only, so the true global total (with TSMC ~$40bn, Samsung, SK Hynix, Alibaba) is materially higher and US-concentration is an overstatement; capex is all PP&E, not AI-only.
